description: A plant, animal, or place is 'waeshi' when it lives or grows outside human cultivation or domestication. A wild rabbit may have little freedom in a damaged habitat, so 'ralu' (free) concerns available action rather than ecological history. An individual animal can become 'woenu' (tame) while its population remains wild.
articulatory_notes: Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', then open into central 'a'. Move the tongue forward into a separate stressed true-mid 'e', keeping the 'a.e' hiatus distinct. Raise the tongue blade towards the postalveolar region for unvoiced 'sh', let the air pass through the narrow channel, and finish with high-front unrounded 'i'.
sound_symbolism: The wide 'a.e' hiatus opens into the sustained airflow of 'sh' before the word narrows to final 'i'. The long outward passage echoes growth continuing beyond a tended boundary.
usage_notes: Noun-position 'waeshi' means wildness. 'waeshi pelowa' is a wild meadow, while 'waeshi tupiwa' is a wild rabbit; danger and the degree of habitat alteration take their own descriptions.

pillars.solarpunk-values: A settlement that leaves room for wild habitat accepts that some living systems will follow cycles outside cultivation.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Wild food and medicine can complement cultivation when gathering leaves enough for the place to renew itself.

semantic_domains.nature: plants, animals, or habitats living outside cultivation or domestication
